about.title: About me
about.paragraph1: My art combines personal biography, theology, and social questions in a figurative and layered visual language.
about.paragraph2: I see art as a space of insight beyond pure rationality that fosters dialogue and connects people, experience, and the search for meaning.

contact.heroSubtitle: I'd love to hear from you. Send me a message and I'll get back to you as soon as possible.

about.heroSubtitle: "Art is not what you see, but what you make others see"
about.storyParagraph1: My name is Bithja, I am 37 years old and was born in November — a Scorpio. Alongside my artistic work, I am studying theology for my state examination. Between art and theology, I keep discovering new spaces for thinking, feeling, and questioning.
about.storyParagraph2: My work often moves between socially critical, political, and theological themes. I am especially interested in questions of identity, body, power, faith, and cultural heritage. Through my studies, I engage deeply with Judaism as well as Ancient Hebrew and Ancient Greek texts — languages in which meanings often run deeper than first impressions suggest.
about.storyParagraph3: Alongside these more philosophical and theological influences, I am also interested in fashion, aesthetics, and body language. Clothing, the body, and staging tell stories about identity, spirit of the times, and social roles. In my artistic work, I use different media: collage, acrylic and oil paint, watercolor, oil and pastel chalk, and pencil drawings. I enjoy working figuratively and realistically, and I focus on anatomy, figure drawing, and nature.
about.storyParagraph4: I also love wordplay, ambiguity, and occasionally youth slang — because for me, art should not only analyze, but also surprise, provoke, and sometimes make you smile.
about.philosophyParagraph1: I am interested not only in what the eyes see, but in what the heart recognizes. For me, reality is not exhausted by the rational or the merely explainable. Art begins where thought, feeling, and experience flow into one another. In this sense, I feel close to ideas from Blaise Pascal. When I speak of the heart, I do not mean mere emotion, but an immediate mode of knowing. Some truths cannot be proven argumentatively — they reveal themselves as inner certainty.
about.philosophyParagraph2: My work therefore does not follow a purely naturalistic claim. It does not aim to simply depict what is already visible, but to make visible what escapes purely intellectual understanding. I do not see the human being as an isolated entity. Here I am especially inspired by Friedrich Schleiermacher, who understood feeling as a fundamental mode of human perception — as awareness of our embeddedness in world, history, relationships, and something greater than ourselves.
about.philosophyParagraph3: For me, art thus becomes a space of resonance in which personal experience, social reality, political questions, and spiritual seeking enter into relationship with one another. Especially in a city like Berlin — shaped by diversity, tensions, and rapid change — I see art as a place of dialogue. Not to harden positions, but to make them visible and negotiable. My works invite people to pause, locate themselves, and enter into exchange — with the image, with others, and with their own stance. Art should not decorate. It should connect.
about.ctaSubheading: Interested in commissioning a piece, collaborating on a project, or learning more about my work?

1.description: Two interlocking hands hold each other in a moment that is both tender and intense. In this touch, soft flowing lines condense infatuation, passion, and the feeling of being completely present with someone for a brief instant. The connection feels almost instinctive—as if they wanted to hold on to a feeling that can hardly be put into words. It is the short, precious peak of closeness in which two people hold each other as if they could stop time itself.
1.idea: The idea for this image grew from observing today’s dating culture, which often feels fast-paced and superficial—shaped by situationships, uncertainty, and ghosting. I asked myself whether deep intimacy, where people truly commit to each other and fully let go, is becoming rarer. The interlocking hands therefore stand for a moment of real trust and safety—for the longing for a closeness that seems to be fading in our time.
1.summary: The painting combines the tender intensity of interlocking hands with the idea that genuine intimacy and emotional safety are being lost in modern dating culture.

2.summary: As a still life, “Kiwi” focuses on the fruit’s reduced presence and suggests that quiet, concentrated beauty can be found in simplicity.

3.description: In “Skin before Sin – When Nudity Had Not Yet Become Judgment,” the female body appears not as an object but as an original presence—a memory of a time before judgment, before shame, morality, and social gazes overloaded the body with meaning. The back faces the viewer and consciously resists being evaluated. Flowing greens, blues, and light tones surround the figure like a quiet, almost paradisiacal space—a moment in which skin was simply skin: free, vulnerable, and human.
3.idea: The idea behind this image stems from the old narrative of Eve, which is still often linked immediately to guilt. For centuries, women in many religious interpretations have carried the image of the sinner—a judgment deeply inscribed into our thinking. This painting tries to make visible a moment before that: a state in which skin was not yet guilt and femininity did not yet have to bear judgment.
3.summary: The work presents the female body as an original, judgment-free presence and links it to the idea of seeing Eve before the historical burden of guilt and shame.

4.description: “Free Iran” carries the pain and longing of a people calling for freedom. It reminds us that political violence affects not only systems but people—their bodies, their voices, and their lives. The image stands for solidarity with those who seek change but are often barely heard, and it is a quiet expression of hope that freedom will one day be stronger than fear.
4.idea: The idea behind “Free Iran” emerged from a feeling of silent emptiness—when the streets stayed quiet and protest was barely visible. This work seeks to show what is often missing: attention, solidarity, and a voice from outside. It understands itself as a quiet protest on canvas, a reminder that freedom is not defended only within a country’s borders. If protest there is barely possible—who raises their voice?
4.summary: “Free Iran” combines a portrait of pain, longing, and hope with the idea of a quiet protest that calls for global attention and solidarity.

5.description: “The Water Up There” shows a clear line: the horizon separating water and sky—just as humanity appears separated from God. Yet in Hebrew, the word for sky (Shamayim) already contains the word for water (Mayim), as if both secretly mirrored one another despite the separation. The frame represents our human need to order and limit the world, but divine reality does not fully fit within that frame—it extends beyond it, quietly and infinitely.
5.idea: “The Water Up There” arose from an observation during my theology studies: in Hebrew, the word for sky (Shamayim) contains the word for water (Mayim). In the image, the horizon clearly divides sky and water—a God-given order that also reveals the distance between God and human beings. Yet language reminds us these worlds are not entirely separate. The frame stands for our need for order—but how permeable is that boundary if the divine still acts in our world?
5.summary: The painting brings together the visible separation of sky and water with the idea that language itself still reveals a deep connection between the divine and human realms.

6.description: “Reset & Reseed” is a thought about new beginnings. Sometimes a society needs a moment to pause—a reset—to remember its roots. Iranian culture carries a long history of diversity, beauty, and strength. Like a seed, this memory still lies in the soil of time. The hope of this image is simple: that what was suppressed can one day grow again—freer, more visible, and more deeply rooted than before.
6.idea: The idea for this image is a political statement, a quiet call for a new beginning. It shows the pomegranate—the national fruit of Iran—as a symbol of origin and memory. Doesn’t everything sometimes need to be reset so what is essential can grow again? Like seeds inside the fruit, Iranian culture still holds the possibility of being replanted—free, alive, and without the oppression of women.
6.summary: “Reset & Reseed” combines the pomegranate as a symbol of collective renewal with the idea of a political reset in which Iranian culture can regrow freely and visibly.

7.description: “Bumble in Berlin” tells of encounters in the fast rhythm of the city. Between hope, curiosity, and the feeling of falling in love, hands find each other for a moment—yet the eyes remain open, always searching for the next possibility. In this dynamic, a closeness arises that can feel intense and beautiful but often lasts only briefly. What remains are fleeting acquaintances and memories of moments that disappear as quickly as they came.
7.idea: The idea for this image emerged from my own “failed dating summer”—a summer full of hope, first infatuation, contradictory signals, and sudden disappearance. Encounters that felt meaningful for a moment, yet remained as fleeting as a swipe on a screen. This fragile dynamic—between closeness and distance, promise and silence—asks a quiet question: if we have endless options today, are we losing exactly what we are actually looking for?
7.summary: The collage condenses the fleeting nature of urban encounters with the idea of a “failed dating summer” in which closeness, hope, and disappearance constantly shift into each other.

8.summary: “Maher” appears as an abstract collage that intentionally leaves the sparse description open while carrying the idea of a fragmented emotional resonance space.

9.summary: “Maher Mama” combines a reduced formal composition with the idea of a quiet, personal narrative about closeness, origin, and memory.

10.description: A comic-inspired composition focused on dynamic line work and motion.
10.summary: “Bike” combines comic-like dynamism and active linework with the idea of translating speed and urban energy into a condensed visual moment.

11.summary: “Lou” is conceived as a sensitive watercolor portrait and follows the idea of making personality visible through light layers of color and restrained form.

12.description: The Walach appears here as a quiet reminder of a culture slowly disappearing from collective memory. In posture, clothing, and vivid colors, the image carries an echo of history, tradition, and identity. At the same time, it asks how we deal with cultural heritage today—what we remember, what we preserve, and what is lost unnoticed in the noise of the present.
12.idea: The idea for this image came through a close friend of mine who is Walach himself. Again and again I see him having to explain who the Walachs are—as if an entire culture had to become visible all over again whenever someone starts telling its story. This painting is therefore also an attempt to give attention: to a history, an identity, and a cultural heritage that is not small—only too rarely seen.
12.summary: “Der Walache” links the portrayal of a threatened cultural memory with the idea of bringing an often-overlooked heritage into focus through personal connection and visibility.

13.description: A half-drawn face between light and shadow reflects the modern desire for perfect beauty while also reminding us that part of our identity always remains hidden. Behind the pursuit of the perfect face, a quiet emptiness grows—as if every stroke of optimization erased something human. The more flawless the surface becomes, the louder the soul asks where it still has space in this image.
13.idea: The idea for this painting emerged as I spent more and more time on social media and saw countless filters, beauty ideals, and the trend of “looksmaxxing.” I asked myself what happens when constant optimization of the exterior becomes ever more important—and what of the actual human being might be lost in the process.
13.summary: The half-drawn face between light and shadow connects the depiction of dehumanizing beauty optimization with the idea that looksmaxxing can erase what is most human.

14.summary: “Hildi” is conceived as a portrait piece and carries the idea of making individuality visible through a quiet and concentrated visual language.

15.summary: “Alucard” relates the graphic precision of the comic genre to the idea of narrating character and tension through clear contours and condensed gesture.

commissions.heroSubtitle: Let's create something uniquely yours. A personal artwork that tells your story.
commissions.journeyTitle: The journey to your unique artwork
commissions.journeySubtitle: A collaborative process from concept to creation

steps.consultationDescription: Every commissioned piece begins with an open conversation. We'll discuss what moves you—thoughts, motifs, images, or life phases seeking expression. My existing works can serve as a starting point. From this dialogue, the foundation for your personal artwork emerges.
steps.conceptDescription: From our conversation, I develop an initial conceptual and visual design. Sketches and visual ideas make visible the direction the work can take. We'll discuss these drafts together, refining motifs, atmosphere, and details until everything feels right. Step by step, a clear vision of your personal artwork emerges.
steps.creationDescription: Once our shared vision is established, the artistic process begins. I work along our design while staying true to my artistic signature. You'll receive progress updates throughout the creation process, ensuring the final piece exceeds your expectations.

investment.title: Investment
investment.descriptionSuffix: . The exact pricing depends on size, complexity, and individual requirements.

timeline.title: Timeline
timeline.descriptionSuffix: , depending on the complexity and size of the artwork.

request.title: Request a commission
request.subtitle: Fill out the form below and I'll get back to you within 24 hours
request.ctaSubheading: Have questions before requesting a commission? Feel free to reach out!

form.name: Full name *
form.descriptionPlaceholder: Tell me about your vision... What inspires you? Which emotions or themes would you like to explore? Are there specific colors, motifs, or existing works of mine that resonate with you?
form.consent: * By submitting this form, you agree to be contacted regarding your artwork request.
form.emailIntro: bithj.a.rts you received a new commission request.

items.question: Can I see the artwork before it's finished?
items.answer: Absolutely! You'll receive progress updates throughout the creation process. After the initial concept approval, I'll share photos at key stages so you can see your artwork coming to life.

items.question: What if I'm not satisfied with the result?
items.answer: Your satisfaction is my priority. We'll work together through the concept and sketch phases to ensure we're aligned on the vision before I begin the final piece. Minor adjustments can be made during the creation process.

items.question: Do you ship internationally?
items.answer: Yes! I carefully package and ship artwork worldwide. Shipping costs will be calculated based on your location and the artwork size, and discussed during our initial consultation.

items.question: Can I commission a piece similar to one I've seen?
items.answer: Yes! My existing works can absolutely serve as inspiration. We'll discuss what elements you love and create something unique while maintaining those aesthetic qualities you admire.
